I have all the information I need. Here is the complete research report:

---

## ISO 7185 Standard Pascal: Complete Built-in Procedures and Functions

### 1. REQUIRED FUNCTIONS

#### A. Arithmetic Functions (Section 6.6.6.2)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `abs(x)` | `abs(x: integer): integer` or `abs(x: real): real` | Returns absolute value; result type matches input type | Compiler intrinsic (single instruction on most architectures) |
| `sqr(x)` | `sqr(x: integer): integer` or `sqr(x: real): real` | Returns x*x; result type matches input type | Compiler intrinsic (emits multiply) |
| `sin(x)` | `sin(x: real): real` | Sine of x (radians) | Runtime library call (needs CORDIC or polynomial approximation) |
| `cos(x)` | `cos(x: real): real` | Cosine of x (radians) | Runtime library call |
| `arctan(x)` | `arctan(x: real): real` | Inverse tangent of x (radians) | Runtime library call |
| `exp(x)` | `exp(x: real): real` | e raised to the power x | Runtime library call |
| `ln(x)` | `ln(x: real): real` | Natural logarithm (x must be positive) | Runtime library call |
| `sqrt(x)` | `sqrt(x: real): real` | Square root (x must be non-negative) | Runtime library call |

#### B. Transfer Functions (Section 6.6.6.3)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `trunc(x)` | `trunc(x: real): integer` | Truncates real toward zero to integer | Compiler intrinsic (float-to-int conversion instruction) |
| `round(x)` | `round(x: real): integer` | Rounds real to nearest integer (banker's rounding: ties go to even) | Compiler intrinsic or thin runtime wrapper |

#### C. Ordinal Functions (Section 6.6.6.4)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `ord(x)` | `ord(x: ordinal): integer` | Returns the ordinal number of any ordinal-type value (char, boolean, enum, integer) | Compiler intrinsic (identity/widening on most implementations) |
| `chr(x)` | `chr(x: integer): char` | Returns the character with ordinal value x | Compiler intrinsic (identity/narrowing) |
| `succ(x)` | `succ(x: ordinal): ordinal` | Returns successor in ordinal sequence (error if x is the last value) | Compiler intrinsic (add 1) |
| `pred(x)` | `pred(x: ordinal): ordinal` | Returns predecessor in ordinal sequence (error if x is the first value) | Compiler intrinsic (subtract 1) |

#### D. Boolean Functions (Section 6.6.6.5)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `odd(x)` | `odd(x: integer): boolean` | Returns true if x is odd | Compiler intrinsic (bitwise AND with 1) |
| `eof(f)` | `eof(f: file): boolean` | Returns true if file position is past the last component; `eof` with no argument defaults to `eof(input)` | Runtime library call (requires file state tracking) |
| `eoln(f)` | `eoln(f: text): boolean` | Returns true if current position is at end-of-line; `eoln` with no argument defaults to `eoln(input)` | Runtime library call (requires file buffer inspection) |

### 2. REQUIRED PROCEDURES

#### E. File Handling Procedures (Section 6.6.5.2)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `reset(f)` | `reset(f: file)` | Opens/resets file for reading; positions at first component; sets `eof(f)` appropriately | Runtime library call |
| `rewrite(f)` | `rewrite(f: file)` | Opens/creates file for writing; empties any existing contents | Runtime library call |
| `get(f)` | `get(f: file)` | Advances file position and loads next component into buffer variable `f^` | Runtime library call |
| `put(f)` | `put(f: file)` | Appends buffer variable `f^` to file; advances position | Runtime library call |

#### F. Text I/O Procedures (Section 6.9)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `read(f, v1, v2, ...)` | Reads values from file f (or `input` if omitted) into variables. For text files: reads integers, reals, chars from character stream with parsing | Compiler intrinsic for dispatch (generates per-type calls); runtime library for actual I/O and parsing |
| `readln(f, v1, ...)` | Like `read`, then skips remainder of current line (up to and including the line terminator) | Same as read, plus line-skip runtime call |
| `write(f, e1, e2, ...)` | Writes expressions to file f (or `output` if omitted). For text files: formats integers, reals, booleans, chars, strings with optional field width `write(x:width)` and decimal places `write(r:width:decimals)` | Compiler intrinsic for dispatch; runtime library for formatting and output |
| `writeln(f, e1, ...)` | Like `write`, then appends a line terminator | Same as write, plus newline runtime call |
| `page(f)` | `page(f: text)` | Advances text file to new page (form feed); `page` with no argument defaults to `page(output)` | Runtime library call (outputs form-feed character) |

#### G. Dynamic Allocation Procedures (Section 6.6.5.3)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `new(p)` | `new(p: pointer)` | Allocates a new variable of the type that p points to; sets p to point to it | Runtime library call (heap allocator) |
| `new(p, c1, c2, ...)` | `new(p: pointer; tag-values...)` | Allocates a variant record with specific variant tag values (only enough space for that variant) | Runtime library call |
| `dispose(p)` | `dispose(p: pointer)` | Deallocates the variable that p points to; p becomes undefined | Runtime library call (heap deallocator) |
| `dispose(p, c1, c2, ...)` | `dispose(p: pointer; tag-values...)` | Deallocates a variant record allocated with matching tag values | Runtime library call |

#### H. Array Packing Procedures (Section 6.6.5.4)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `pack(a, i, z)` | `pack(a: unpacked-array; i: index; var z: packed-array)` | Copies elements from unpacked array `a` starting at index `i` into packed array `z` | Compiler intrinsic or runtime call (depends on whether packed arrays differ from unpacked in the implementation) |
| `unpack(z, a, i)` | `unpack(z: packed-array; var a: unpacked-array; i: index)` | Copies elements from packed array `z` into unpacked array `a` starting at index `i` | Same as pack |

### 3. REQUIRED CONSTANTS AND TYPES

| Name | Kind | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| `maxint` | Constant | Maximum value of type integer |
| `true`, `false` | Constants | Boolean values |
| `integer` | Type | Signed integer type |
| `real` | Type | Floating-point type |
| `boolean` | Type | Logical type (false, true) |
| `char` | Type | Character type (ISO 646 or implementation-defined character set) |
| `text` | Type | File of char with line structure |
| `input` | Variable | Standard input text file (program parameter) |
| `output` | Variable | Standard output text file (program parameter) |

---

### 4. COMMON BEGINNER PROGRAMS AND THEIR RUNTIME NEEDS

| Program | Description | Runtime Support Required |
|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Hello World** | `writeln('Hello, World!')` | `write` (string output), `writeln` (newline) |
| **Factorial** (recursive) | Recursive function, `writeln(fact(n))` | `write_int` (integer output), `writeln`, function call/stack support (compiler) |
| **Factorial** (iterative) | For loop with multiplication | Same as above; loop is compiler-generated |
| **Fibonacci** | Recursive or iterative sequence generation | `write_int`, `writeln`; no special runtime beyond I/O |
| **Read and echo** | `read(x); writeln(x)` | `read_int` or `read_char` (input parsing), `write_int`, `writeln` |
| **Temperature conversion** | Real arithmetic, formatted output | `read` (real), `write(r:w:d)` (real formatting with field width and decimal places), `writeln` |
| **Array sorting** (bubble/insertion) | Array indexing, comparison, swap | Array bounds checking (`_p24p_bounds_check`), no special runtime if bounds checking is inline |
| **String manipulation** | `packed array [1..N] of char` operations | `pack`/`unpack` (if mixed packed/unpacked), string comparison (compiler-generated or runtime), `write` for packed char arrays |
| **Linked list** | Dynamic nodes with pointers | `new(p)` (heap allocation), `dispose(p)` (heap deallocation), pointer nil-checking (`_p24p_nil_check`) |
| **Binary tree** | Recursive pointer-based structure | `new`, `dispose`, recursion (compiler stack), nil checks |
| **File copy** | Read from one file, write to another | `reset`, `rewrite`, `get`, `put`, `eof`, full file I/O runtime |
| **Text processing** | Read lines, count words/chars | `read`/`readln` (char/line input), `eoln`, `eof`, character classification (no standard library for this -- must be hand-coded via ord comparisons) |
| **Set operations** | `type charset = set of char` with `in`, `+`, `*`, `-` | Set operations are compiler-generated (bitfield ops), but runtime may provide set-copy/set-compare helpers for large sets |
| **Case statement** | Pattern matching on ordinal values | Compiler-generated jump table; no runtime needed |

### 5. RUNTIME SUPPORT CATEGORIES SUMMARY

**Things that are purely compiler intrinsics (no runtime needed):**
- `abs`, `sqr`, `odd` -- single arithmetic instructions
- `ord`, `chr`, `succ`, `pred` -- identity or +1/-1 operations
- `trunc`, `round` -- float-to-int conversion
- Boolean operators (`and`, `or`, `not`) -- bitwise ops
- Set operations (`in`, `+`, `*`, `-`) -- bitfield ops
- Array indexing, record field access -- address arithmetic

**Things that need runtime library support:**
- All I/O: `write` (integer/real/boolean/char/string formatting), `read` (integer/real/char parsing), `writeln`, `readln`, `page`, `get`, `put`, `reset`, `rewrite`
- File state: `eof`, `eoln`
- Heap management: `new`, `dispose`
- Math functions requiring algorithms: `sin`, `cos`, `arctan`, `exp`, `ln`, `sqrt`
- Array packing: `pack`, `unpack` (if packed representation differs)
- Runtime checks: bounds checking, nil-pointer checking, subrange checking, variant record tag checking

**Things that are borderline (could be either):**
- `pack`/`unpack` -- often no-ops if packed=unpacked in the implementation
- `round` -- could be an instruction or a small helper
- String comparison -- compiler can inline or call a runtime memcmp-like routine

---

That is the complete set of ISO 7185 Standard Pascal required built-in identifiers. The standard defines exactly **17 required functions** (`abs`, `sqr`, `sin`, `cos`, `arctan`, `exp`, `ln`, `sqrt`, `trunc`, `round`, `ord`, `chr`, `succ`, `pred`, `odd`, `eof`, `eoln`) and **13 required procedures** (`reset`, `rewrite`, `get`, `put`, `read`, `readln`, `write`, `writeln`, `page`, `new`, `dispose`, `pack`, `unpack`), plus the constant `maxint`, the types `integer`, `real`, `boolean`, `char`, `text`, and the file variables `input` and `output`.

Sources:
- [Standard Pascal - Free Pascal wiki](https://wiki.freepascal.org/Standard_Pascal)

- [Pascal ISO 7185:1990 PDF](https://www.standardpascal.org/iso7185.pdf)
- [Pascal FAQ](https://www.standardpascaline.org/pascalfaq.html)

1. The ISO 7185 Standard Pascal required built-in procedures and functions (the complete list)
2. Simple Pascal example programs that beginners write (things like factorial, fibonacci, string manipulation, file I/O, linked lists, sorting arrays, etc.)

content.text:
I have all the information I need. Here is the complete research report:

---

## ISO 7185 Standard Pascal: Complete Built-in Procedures and Functions

### 1. REQUIRED FUNCTIONS

#### A. Arithmetic Functions (Section 6.6.6.2)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `abs(x)` | `abs(x: integer): integer` or `abs(x: real): real` | Returns absolute value; result type matches input type | Compiler intrinsic (single instruction on most architectures) |
| `sqr(x)` | `sqr(x: integer): integer` or `sqr(x: real): real` | Returns x*x; result type matches input type | Compiler intrinsic (emits multiply) |
| `sin(x)` | `sin(x: real): real` | Sine of x (radians) | Runtime library call (needs CORDIC or polynomial approximation) |
| `cos(x)` | `cos(x: real): real` | Cosine of x (radians) | Runtime library call |
| `arctan(x)` | `arctan(x: real): real` | Inverse tangent of x (radians) | Runtime library call |
| `exp(x)` | `exp(x: real): real` | e raised to the power x | Runtime library call |
| `ln(x)` | `ln(x: real): real` | Natural logarithm (x must be positive) | Runtime library call |
| `sqrt(x)` | `sqrt(x: real): real` | Square root (x must be non-negative) | Runtime library call |

#### B. Transfer Functions (Section 6.6.6.3)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `trunc(x)` | `trunc(x: real): integer` | Truncates real toward zero to integer | Compiler intrinsic (float-to-int conversion instruction) |
| `round(x)` | `round(x: real): integer` | Rounds real to nearest integer (banker's rounding: ties go to even) | Compiler intrinsic or thin runtime wrapper |

#### C. Ordinal Functions (Section 6.6.6.4)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `ord(x)` | `ord(x: ordinal): integer` | Returns the ordinal number of any ordinal-type value (char, boolean, enum, integer) | Compiler intrinsic (identity/widening on most implementations) |
| `chr(x)` | `chr(x: integer): char` | Returns the character with ordinal value x | Compiler intrinsic (identity/narrowing) |
| `succ(x)` | `succ(x: ordinal): ordinal` | Returns successor in ordinal sequence (error if x is the last value) | Compiler intrinsic (add 1) |
| `pred(x)` | `pred(x: ordinal): ordinal` | Returns predecessor in ordinal sequence (error if x is the first value) | Compiler intrinsic (subtract 1) |

#### D. Boolean Functions (Section 6.6.6.5)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `odd(x)` | `odd(x: integer): boolean` | Returns true if x is odd | Compiler intrinsic (bitwise AND with 1) |
| `eof(f)` | `eof(f: file): boolean` | Returns true if file position is past the last component; `eof` with no argument defaults to `eof(input)` | Runtime library call (requires file state tracking) |
| `eoln(f)` | `eoln(f: text): boolean` | Returns true if current position is at end-of-line; `eoln` with no argument defaults to `eoln(input)` | Runtime library call (requires file buffer inspection) |

### 2. REQUIRED PROCEDURES

#### E. File Handling Procedures (Section 6.6.5.2)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `reset(f)` | `reset(f: file)` | Opens/resets file for reading; positions at first component; sets `eof(f)` appropriately | Runtime library call |
| `rewrite(f)` | `rewrite(f: file)` | Opens/creates file for writing; empties any existing contents | Runtime library call |
| `get(f)` | `get(f: file)` | Advances file position and loads next component into buffer variable `f^` | Runtime library call |
| `put(f)` | `put(f: file)` | Appends buffer variable `f^` to file; advances position | Runtime library call |

#### F. Text I/O Procedures (Section 6.9)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `read(f, v1, v2, ...)` | Reads values from file f (or `input` if omitted) into variables. For text files: reads integers, reals, chars from character stream with parsing | Compiler intrinsic for dispatch (generates per-type calls); runtime library for actual I/O and parsing |
| `readln(f, v1, ...)` | Like `read`, then skips remainder of current line (up to and including the line terminator) | Same as read, plus line-skip runtime call |
| `write(f, e1, e2, ...)` | Writes expressions to file f (or `output` if omitted). For text files: formats integers, reals, booleans, chars, strings with optional field width `write(x:width)` and decimal places `write(r:width:decimals)` | Compiler intrinsic for dispatch; runtime library for formatting and output |
| `writeln(f, e1, ...)` | Like `write`, then appends a line terminator | Same as write, plus newline runtime call |
| `page(f)` | `page(f: text)` | Advances text file to new page (form feed); `page` with no argument defaults to `page(output)` | Runtime library call (outputs form-feed character) |

#### G. Dynamic Allocation Procedures (Section 6.6.5.3)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `new(p)` | `new(p: pointer)` | Allocates a new variable of the type that p points to; sets p to point to it | Runtime library call (heap allocator) |
| `new(p, c1, c2, ...)` | `new(p: pointer; tag-values...)` | Allocates a variant record with specific variant tag values (only enough space for that variant) | Runtime library call |
| `dispose(p)` | `dispose(p: pointer)` | Deallocates the variable that p points to; p becomes undefined | Runtime library call (heap deallocator) |
| `dispose(p, c1, c2, ...)` | `dispose(p: pointer; tag-values...)` | Deallocates a variant record allocated with matching tag values | Runtime library call |

#### H. Array Packing Procedures (Section 6.6.5.4)

| Name | Signature | Description | Intrinsic or Runtime? |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| `pack(a, i, z)` | `pack(a: unpacked-array; i: index; var z: packed-array)` | Copies elements from unpacked array `a` starting at index `i` into packed array `z` | Compiler intrinsic or runtime call (depends on whether packed arrays differ from unpacked in the implementation) |
| `unpack(z, a, i)` | `unpack(z: packed-array; var a: unpacked-array; i: index)` | Copies elements from packed array `z` into unpacked array `a` starting at index `i` | Same as pack |

### 3. REQUIRED CONSTANTS AND TYPES

| Name | Kind | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| `maxint` | Constant | Maximum value of type integer |
| `true`, `false` | Constants | Boolean values |
| `integer` | Type | Signed integer type |
| `real` | Type | Floating-point type |
| `boolean` | Type | Logical type (false, true) |
| `char` | Type | Character type (ISO 646 or implementation-defined character set) |
| `text` | Type | File of char with line structure |
| `input` | Variable | Standard input text file (program parameter) |
| `output` | Variable | Standard output text file (program parameter) |

---

### 4. COMMON BEGINNER PROGRAMS AND THEIR RUNTIME NEEDS

| Program | Description | Runtime Support Required |
|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Hello World** | `writeln('Hello, World!')` | `write` (string output), `writeln` (newline) |
| **Factorial** (recursive) | Recursive function, `writeln(fact(n))` | `write_int` (integer output), `writeln`, function call/stack support (compiler) |
| **Factorial** (iterative) | For loop with multiplication | Same as above; loop is compiler-generated |
| **Fibonacci** | Recursive or iterative sequence generation | `write_int`, `writeln`; no special runtime beyond I/O |
| **Read and echo** | `read(x); writeln(x)` | `read_int` or `read_char` (input parsing), `write_int`, `writeln` |
| **Temperature conversion** | Real arithmetic, formatted output | `read` (real), `write(r:w:d)` (real formatting with field width and decimal places), `writeln` |
| **Array sorting** (bubble/insertion) | Array indexing, comparison, swap | Array bounds checking (`_p24p_bounds_check`), no special runtime if bounds checking is inline |
| **String manipulation** | `packed array [1..N] of char` operations | `pack`/`unpack` (if mixed packed/unpacked), string comparison (compiler-generated or runtime), `write` for packed char arrays |
| **Linked list** | Dynamic nodes with pointers | `new(p)` (heap allocation), `dispose(p)` (heap deallocation), pointer nil-checking (`_p24p_nil_check`) |
| **Binary tree** | Recursive pointer-based structure | `new`, `dispose`, recursion (compiler stack), nil checks |
| **File copy** | Read from one file, write to another | `reset`, `rewrite`, `get`, `put`, `eof`, full file I/O runtime |
| **Text processing** | Read lines, count words/chars | `read`/`readln` (char/line input), `eoln`, `eof`, character classification (no standard library for this -- must be hand-coded via ord comparisons) |
| **Set operations** | `type charset = set of char` with `in`, `+`, `*`, `-` | Set operations are compiler-generated (bitfield ops), but runtime may provide set-copy/set-compare helpers for large sets |
| **Case statement** | Pattern matching on ordinal values | Compiler-generated jump table; no runtime needed |

### 5. RUNTIME SUPPORT CATEGORIES SUMMARY

**Things that are purely compiler intrinsics (no runtime needed):**
- `abs`, `sqr`, `odd` -- single arithmetic instructions
- `ord`, `chr`, `succ`, `pred` -- identity or +1/-1 operations
- `trunc`, `round` -- float-to-int conversion
- Boolean operators (`and`, `or`, `not`) -- bitwise ops
- Set operations (`in`, `+`, `*`, `-`) -- bitfield ops
- Array indexing, record field access -- address arithmetic

**Things that need runtime library support:**
- All I/O: `write` (integer/real/boolean/char/string formatting), `read` (integer/real/char parsing), `writeln`, `readln`, `page`, `get`, `put`, `reset`, `rewrite`
- File state: `eof`, `eoln`
- Heap management: `new`, `dispose`
- Math functions requiring algorithms: `sin`, `cos`, `arctan`, `exp`, `ln`, `sqrt`
- Array packing: `pack`, `unpack` (if packed representation differs)
- Runtime checks: bounds checking, nil-pointer checking, subrange checking, variant record tag checking

**Things that are borderline (could be either):**
- `pack`/`unpack` -- often no-ops if packed=unpacked in the implementation
- `round` -- could be an instruction or a small helper
- String comparison -- compiler can inline or call a runtime memcmp-like routine

---

That is the complete set of ISO 7185 Standard Pascal required built-in identifiers. The standard defines exactly **17 required functions** (`abs`, `sqr`, `sin`, `cos`, `arctan`, `exp`, `ln`, `sqrt`, `trunc`, `round`, `ord`, `chr`, `succ`, `pred`, `odd`, `eof`, `eoln`) and **13 required procedures** (`reset`, `rewrite`, `get`, `put`, `read`, `readln`, `write`, `writeln`, `page`, `new`, `dispose`, `pack`, `unpack`), plus the constant `maxint`, the types `integer`, `real`, `boolean`, `char`, `text`, and the file variables `input` and `output`.

Sources:
- [Standard Pascal - Free Pascal wiki](https://wiki.freepascal.org/Standard_Pascal)
- [Rules of ISO 7185 PASCAL](https://www.standardpascaline.org/iso7185rules.html)
- [ANSI-ISO PASCAL (moorecad mirror)](https://www.moorecad.com/standardpascal/iso7185.html)
- [Pascal ISO 7185:1990 PDF](https://www.standardpascal.org/iso7185.pdf)
- [Pascal FAQ](https://www.standardpascaline.org/pascalfaq.html)

Here's the full picture.

## What We Have (21 routines)

| Category | Routines | Status |
|----------|----------|--------|
| **Output** | `write_int`, `write_bool`, `write_ln`, `write_str` | Done |
| **Std functions** | `abs`, `odd`, `ord`, `chr`, `succ`, `pred`, `sqr` | Done |
| **Safety checks** | `bounds_check`, `nil_check` | Done |
| **Input** | `read_int`, `read_char`, `read_ln` | Done |
| **Heap** | `heap_init`, `new`, `dispose`, `leak_report` | Done |

## What's Missing vs ISO 7185 Standard Pascal

### Stage 1 — Write Formatting (high value, enables most demo programs)

These are the most impactful gaps. Almost every Pascal textbook example uses formatted output.

| Routine | Why | Example Pascal |
|---------|-----|----------------|
| `_p24p_write_char` | `write(chr(65))` or `write(c)` — we have no char output separate from strings | `write('A')` |
| `_p24p_write_int_field` | `write(x:8)` — right-justified in field width. Very common in Pascal. | `write(n:6)` |
| `_p24p_write_char_field` | `write(c:5)` — char in field width | `write(ch:3)` |
| `_p24p_write_bool_field` | `write(b:8)` — boolean in field width | `write(flag:6)` |
| `_p24p_write_str_field` | `write(s:20)` — string in field width | `write(name:15)` |

**Test programs this unlocks:** Multiplication tables, formatted reports, column-aligned output.

### Stage 2 — Subrange & EOF (enables real input programs)

| Routine | Why |
|---------|-----|
| `_p24p_subrange_check` | Like bounds_check but for subrange types (`type month = 1..12`) |
| `_p24p_eof` | `eof` / `eof(input)` — test if UART input is exhausted (check for EOT byte 0x04) |
| `_p24p_eoln` | `eoln` / `eoln(input)` — test if current position is at end-of-line (peek for LF) |
| `_p24p_read_bool` | `read(b)` where b is boolean — parse TRUE/FALSE from input |

**Test programs this unlocks:** File-processing loops (`while not eof do`), robust input validation.

### Stage 3 — Sets & Strings (already planned, next agentrail step)

| Routine | Why |
|---------|-----|
| `_p24p_set_union` | `s1 + s2` — bitwise OR of set words |
| `_p24p_set_intersect` | `s1 * s2` — bitwise AND |
| `_p24p_set_diff` | `s1 - s2` — bitwise AND-NOT |
| `_p24p_set_member` | `x in s` — test bit |
| `_p24p_str_compare` | `if s1 < s2` — lexicographic comparison of packed char arrays |
| `_p24p_str_copy` | Assignment of packed char arrays |
| `_p24p_str_concat` | `s1 + s2` (if supported) |

**Test programs this unlocks:** Character classification (`if ch in ['a'..'z']`), string sorting.

### Stage 4 — Math Functions (only needed for real/float programs)

These require a `real` type which the COR24 24-bit VM likely won't support natively. **Defer or skip entirely** unless floating point is added to the VM.

| Routine | Notes |
|---------|-------|
| `sin`, `cos`, `arctan` | Trig — needs float or fixed-point |
| `exp`, `ln`, `sqrt` | Transcendentals — needs float or fixed-point |
| `trunc`, `round` | Float-to-int conversion |

### Stage 5 — File I/O (likely out of scope for embedded target)

Standard Pascal has a full file abstraction (`reset`, `rewrite`, `get`, `put`). On a UART-only embedded VM, this maps poorly. **Skip unless a filesystem is added.**

| Routine | Notes |
|---------|-------|
| `reset`, `rewrite` | File open — no filesystem on COR24 |
| `get`, `put` | File read/write — UART only has sys 1/2 |
| `page` | Form feed — trivial (`push 12; sys 1`) but rarely needed |

### Stage 6 — Pack/Unpack (likely no-op)

`pack(a,i,z)` and `unpack(z,a,i)` copy between packed and unpacked arrays. On this VM, packed = unpacked (everything is word-sized), so these are just `memcpy` equivalents. **Implement only if the compiler generates calls to them.**

## Sample Pascal Programs That Would Test Each Stage

```pascal
{ Stage 1 test: Multiplication table }
program MultTable;
var i, j: integer;
begin
  for i := 1 to 9 do begin
    for j := 1 to 9 do
      write(i*j:4);
    writeln
  end
end.

{ Stage 2 test: Sum numbers until EOF }
program SumInput;
var n, total: integer;
begin
  total := 0;
  while not eof do begin
    read(n);
    total := total + n
  end;
  writeln(total)
end.

{ Stage 3 test: Classify characters }
program CharClass;
var ch: char;
begin
  read(ch);
  if ch in ['A'..'Z', 'a'..'z'] then writeln('LETTER')
  else if ch in ['0'..'9'] then writeln('DIGIT')
  else writeln('OTHER')
end.

{ Already works today: Factorial }
program Factorial;
var n, f, i: integer;
begin
  read(n);
  f := 1;
  for i := 2 to n do
    f := f * i;
  writeln(f)
end.

{ Already works today: Linked list }
program LinkedList;
type pnode = ^node; node = record val: integer; next: pnode end;
var head, p: pnode;
begin
  new(head); head^.val := 10; head^.next := nil;
  new(p); p^.val := 20; p^.next := head; head := p;
  { traverse and print }
  p := head;
  while p <> nil do begin writeln(p^.val); p := p^.next end;
  { cleanup }
  dispose(head^.next); dispose(head)
end.
```

## Recommended Priority

**Stage 1 (write formatting) is the biggest bang-for-buck.** Field-width output is in virtually every Pascal textbook program, and `write_char` is a trivial gap (just `sys 1`). Stage 2 (eof/eoln) is needed for any input-processing program. Stage 3 (sets/strings) is already your next agentrail step. Stages 4-6 can likely be deferred indefinitely given the embedded target.

content.content:
d adds gp — accessing memory far beyond globals_seg. Additionally, `relocate_data_refs()` has a boundary check `val < limit + load_addr` (should be `val < limit`) which incorrectly catches global-segment references and relocates them as if they were data refs. |
| Impact | Any .spc using `loadg`/`storeg` with globals TRAPs immediately (TRAP 4 or TRAP 6). Workaround: use `push symbol` / `store` / `load` pattern (absolute addressing) or use local variables (`storel`/`loadl`). web-p24c Countdown demo uses locals as workaround. |
| Suggested fix | (1) For `loadg`/`storeg`: emit raw word index (0, 1, 2...) not `code_size + data_size + index`. (2) For `relocate_data_refs()`: change `val < limit + load_addr` to `val < limit` to exclude globals from data relocation. |
| **pa24r response** | Both fixes applied. (1) Global symbols stay as raw byte offsets in symbol table; resolve_operand now takes global_base param — loadg/storeg/addrg pass None (word index = value/3), push passes Some(code_size+data_size). (2) relocate_data_refs boundary fixed to `val < limit`. 52 tests pass. |
REQ-002 (pr24p metadata) — VERIFIED by pl24r 2026-03-26: real runtime.spc links cleanly, 3 exports detected, all externs resolve

### REQ-011: web-p24c needs Hello World with words demo

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| From | web-p24c |
| To | p24p |
| Status | **DONE** |
| Completed | 2026-03-26 |
| Priority | High |
| Requested | 2026-03-26 |
| Detail | web-p24c is a browser-based Pascal IDE displaying .pas + .spc side by side, running via pl24r + pa24r + pvm.s in WASM. Our current demos only support writeln(42). We want a Hello World that prints words: writeln('Hello, World!'). Questions for p24p: (1) What is the status of string literal codegen? (2) What .spc will p24p emit for writeln('...')? We expect .data with string bytes + push addr + call _p24p_write_str. (3) Can you provide a hand-written .spc example of Hello World with string output that we can embed now? We can link it with pr24p runtime via pl24r. |
| Blocked by | REQ-009 (_p24p_write_str in pr24p) |
| Notes | web-p24c embeds .pas + .spc pairs via include_str!(). Linking (pl24r) and assembly (pa24r) happen at runtime in WASM. If p24p can share the expected .spc format for string writeln, we can hand-write a demo .spc immediately. |

(2) Planned .spc: `.data S0 72,101,...,0` + `push S0` / `call _p24p_write_str` / `call _p24p_write_ln`. toolUseResult.stdout:
d adds gp — accessing memory far beyond globals_seg. Additionally, `relocate_data_refs()` has a boundary check `val < limit + load_addr` (should be `val < limit`) which incorrectly catches global-segment references and relocates them as if they were data refs. |
| Impact | Any .spc using `loadg`/`storeg` with globals TRAPs immediately (TRAP 4 or TRAP 6). Workaround: use `push symbol` / `store` / `load` pattern (absolute addressing) or use local variables (`storel`/`loadl`). web-p24c Countdown demo uses locals as workaround. |
| Suggested fix | (1) For `loadg`/`storeg`: emit raw word index (0, 1, 2...) not `code_size + data_size + index`. (2) For `relocate_data_refs()`: change `val < limit + load_addr` to `val < limit` to exclude globals from data relocation. |
| **pa24r response** | Both fixes applied. (1) Global symbols stay as raw byte offsets in symbol table; resolve_operand now takes global_base param — loadg/storeg/addrg pass None (word index = value/3), push passes Some(code_size+data_size). (2) relocate_data_refs boundary fixed to `val < limit`. 52 tests pass. |

=== REQ-023 ===
### REQ-023: p24p implement Pascal uses clause and unit support

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| From | web-dv24r (coordinator) |
| To | p24p |
| Status | **REQUESTED** |
| Priority | High |
| Requested | 2026-03-27 |
| Detail | Implement `uses` clause so Pascal programs can import library units. This enables proper LED support (and future hardware/system libraries) without hardcoded builtins. Scope: (1) Parse `uses UnitName;` or `uses Unit1, Unit2;` after `program` declaration. (2) Map unit names to .spc files (e.g., `uses Hardware` → look for `hardware.spc` in a search path or known location). (3) Emit `.extern` only for symbols from used units (currently emits all externs unconditionally). (4) Unit .spc files are provided by pr24p with `.module UnitName` / `.export` / `.endmodule` metadata — pl24r already handles linking. Example target: `program Blink; uses Hardware; begin SetLED(1) end.` where Hardware unit provides `SetLED` → `_p24p_set_led` → `sys 3`. |
| Blocked by | — |
| Notes | Once p24p supports `uses`, file a follow-up REQ for pr24p to create Hardware and System units. The existing runtime.spc becomes the System unit (auto-imported). Hardware unit adds SetLED, ReadSwitch, etc. |

content.text:
Good to see p24p Phase 6 landed. String literals, for loops, `write()`, and char literals are exactly the features that unlock most of the demo programs we discussed.

**Where things stand now:**
- p24p can compile `writeln('Hello, World!')`, `write(n:6)`, `for` loops, char literals
- pr24p runtime has all 21 routines those features need (`write_str`, `write_int`, `write_ln`, etc.)
- REQ-023 (`uses` clause) and REQ-024 (Hardware/System units) remain open for the next phase

The `write_char` and field-width formatting routines I identified earlier (Stage 1 from our gap analysis) are now more relevant — p24p can emit `write(c)` and `write(x:8)` but the runtime doesn't yet have `_p24p_write_char` or `_p24p_write_int_field`. Those would be good candidates for our next agentrail step after sets/strings, or could be squeezed in if the compiler starts generating calls to them.

Want me to proceed with the next agentrail step (008 — sets/strings), or pivot to adding the write formatting routines that p24p's new features would exercise?
Let me first check what p24p actually emits for `write(c)` and `write(x:8)` so I know exactly what runtime calls to implement.

2. Check if it handles field-width formatting like `write(x:8)` — does it emit a call to a field-width variant, or does it just call the basic write_int?

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have comprehensive information. Let me create a final summary.

## Summary: p24p Compiler Write/Writeln Code Generation

Based on my exploration of the p24p compiler at `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p`, here are the exact findings:

### **Runtime Function Names Emitted**

The compiler emits `call` instructions to these runtime functions:

| Type | Runtime Function | Stack Behavior |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Integer** | `_p24p_write_int` | `( n -- )` — pops integer, prints to UART |
| **Boolean** | `_p24p_write_bool` | `( b -- )` — pops boolean (0/1), prints "FALSE"/"TRUE" |
| **String** | `_p24p_write_str` | `( addr -- )` — pops string address, prints null-terminated string |
| **Newline** | `_p24p_write_ln` | `( -- )` — emits newline (CR LF or LF) |

**Phase 1 (planned, not yet in Phase 0):**
- `_p24p_write_int_w` — `( n width -- )` right-justify integer in width columns

### **Source File Location**

The codegen logic is in `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p/src/parser.c`:
- **parse_write_stmt()** at lines 439–471
- **parse_writeln_stmt()** at lines 473–506

### **Field-Width Formatting**

**Phase 0 (current):** **NOT IMPLEMENTED** — The compiler does NOT handle field-width syntax like `write(x:8)`. It will parse the colon as an error.

**Phase 1 (in planning docs at `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p/docs/phase1-spec.md` lines 510–523):** Field widths will be supported with syntax like `write(n:5)`, which will compile to:
```
    loadl 0               ; n
    push 5                ; width
    call _p24p_write_int_w
```

### **Char Literal Handling**

Char literals (e.g., `write('A')` or `write(' ')`) are **NOT treated as a distinct type**. Instead:
1. When the parser encounters a `TOK_CHAR_LIT` (single-quoted character), it converts it to a 1-character string
2. It stores the character in the string table at `str_count` index
3. It emits `push S<index>` (string data reference)
4. The expression type becomes `TYPE_STRING`
5. The write call becomes `call _p24p_write_str` (not `write_char`)

**See lines 112–127 in parser.c:**
```c
if (tok_type == TOK_CHAR_LIT) {
    /* Treat char literal as a 1-char string for write/writeln */
    idx = str_count;
    // ... store character as 1-byte string ...
    printf("    push S%d\n", idx);
    return TYPE_STRING;  // returns as string type
}
```

There is **NO `_p24p_write_char`** runtime function in Phase 0. (Phase 1 spec mentions it at line 627 but not yet implemented.)

### **Grammar & Supported Features**

From `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p/docs/pascal-subset.md`:

**Current (Phase 0):**
- `write-stmt = "write" [ "(" expr-list ")" ]` — no field-width syntax
- `writeln-stmt = "writeln" [ "(" expr-list ")" ]` — same, plus newline
- `expr-list = expression { "," expression }` — comma-separated expressions only

**Literals supported:**
- Integer: `42` → `call _p24p_write_int`
- Boolean: `true`/`false` → `call _p24p_write_bool`
- String: `"Hello"` → `call _p24p_write_str`
- Char: `'A'` → converted to 1-char string → `call _p24p_write_str`

### **Recent Implementation (Commit b710635)**

The write() statement (without newline) was added in commit **b710635** (Mar 27, 2026) alongside string literals and char literals. All three features went in together. Test file: `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p/tests/t09_write.pas` demonstrates the feature.

### **Module Metadata Emitted**

At program start, the parser emits extern declarations (lines 743–746):
```c
printf(".extern _p24p_write_int\n");
printf(".extern _p24p_write_bool\n");
printf(".extern _p24p_write_str\n");
printf(".extern _p24p_write_ln\n");
```

```c
    // ... store character as 1-byte string ...
    printf("    push S%d\n", idx);
    return TYPE_STRING;  // returns as string type
}
```

There is **NO `_p24p_write_char`** runtime function in Phase 0. (Phase 1 spec mentions it at line 627 but not yet implemented.)

### **Grammar & Supported Features**

From `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p/docs/pascal-subset.md`:

**Current (Phase 0):**
- `write-stmt = "write" [ "(" expr-list ")" ]` — no field-width syntax
- `writeln-stmt = "writeln" [ "(" expr-list ")" ]` — same, plus newline
- `expr-list = expression { "," expression }` — comma-separated expressions only

**Literals supported:**
- Integer: `42` → `call _p24p_write_int`
- Boolean: `true`/`false` → `call _p24p_write_bool`
- String: `"Hello"` → `call _p24p_write_str`
- Char: `'A'` → converted to 1-char string → `call _p24p_write_str`

### **Recent Implementation (Commit b710635)**

The write() statement (without newline) was added in commit **b710635** (Mar 27, 2026) alongside string literals and char literals. All three features went in together. Test file: `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/p24p/tests/t09_write.pas` demonstrates the feature.

### **Module Metadata Emitted**

At program start, the parser emits extern declarations (lines 743–746):
```c
printf(".extern _p24p_write_int\n");
printf(".extern _p24p_write_bool\n");
printf(".extern _p24p_write_str\n");
printf(".extern _p24p_write_ln\n");
```

Good. The compiler currently calls exactly 4 write routines: `_p24p_write_int`, `_p24p_write_bool`, `_p24p_write_str`, `_p24p_write_ln`. Char literals become 1-char strings via `write_str`. Field-width (`write(x:8)`) is planned for Phase 1 as `_p24p_write_int_w`.

So the immediate value-add for pr24p is:

1. **`_p24p_write_char`** — direct char output (more efficient than 1-char string, ready for when p24p Phase 1 adds char type distinction)
2. **`_p24p_write_int_w`** — right-justified integer in field width (p24p Phase 1 spec already names this exact function)
3. **`_p24p_eof`** — enables `while not eof do` input loops
4. **`_p24p_eoln`** — enables line-oriented input processing
5. **`_p24p_subrange_check`** — for subrange types

These are all small routines. Let me start.

.module runtime
.export _p24p_write_int
.export _p24p_write_bool
.export _p24p_write_ln
.export _p24p_write_str
.export _p24p_abs
.export _p24p_odd
.export _p24p_ord
.export _p24p_chr
.export _p24p_succ
.export _p24p_pred
.export _p24p_sqr
.export _p24p_bounds_check
.export _p24p_nil_check
.export _p24p_read_int
.export _p24p_read_char
.export _p24p_read_ln
.export _p24p_heap_init
.export _p24p_new
.export _p24p_dispose
.export _p24p_leak_report

; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; n '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
wi_print:
    dup
    jz wi_done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp wi_print
wi_done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_bool ( b -- )
; Print TRUE or FALSE to UART.
; Compares argument to 0: if zero prints FALSE, otherwise prints TRUE.
; Each character is pushed and output via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_bool 0
    loada 0              ; load argument b
    jz wb_false
    ; print "TRUE"
    push 84              ; 'T'
    sys 1
    push 82              ; 'R'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
    jmp wb_done
wb_false:
    ; print "FALSE"
    push 70              ; 'F'
    sys 1
    push 65              ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
wb_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_ln ( -- )
; Print newline (LF) to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_ln 0
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_write_str ( addr -- )
; Print null-terminated string from data segment to UART.
; Walks bytes via loadb, calls sys 1 (PUTC) for each until null.
.proc _p24p_write_str 1
    loada 0              ; load string address
    storel 0             ; local0 = pointer
ws_loop:
    loadl 0              ; load pointer
    loadb                ; load byte at pointer
    dup
    jz ws_done           ; null terminator -> done
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0             ; pointer++
    jmp ws_loop
ws_done:
    drop                 ; discard null byte
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Standard functions
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Will be replaced with Pascal-compiled versions when p24c Phase 1 lands.

; _p24p_abs ( x -- |x| )
; Absolute value. Returns x if x >= 0, else -x.
.proc _p24p_abs 0
    loada 0              ; load x
    dup
    push 0
    lt                   ; x < 0?
    jz abs_done
    neg                  ; negate if negative
abs_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_odd ( x -- x mod 2 <> 0 )
; Returns true (1) if x is odd, false (0) if even.
.proc _p24p_odd 0
    loada 0              ; load x
    push 2
    mod                  ; x mod 2
    push 0
    ne                   ; result != 0 -> true
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_ord ( c -- c )
; Character to integer. Identity on this VM (chars are integers).
.proc _p24p_ord 0
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_chr ( n -- n )
; Integer to character. Identity on this VM (chars are integers).
.proc _p24p_chr 0
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_succ ( x -- x+1 )
; Next ordinal value.
.proc _p24p_succ 0
    loada 0
    push 1
    add
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_pred ( x -- x-1 )
; Previous ordinal value.
.proc _p24p_pred 0
    loada 0
    push 1
    sub
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_sqr ( x -- x*x )
; Square of integer.
.proc _p24p_sqr 0
    loada 0
    dup
    mul
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Runtime checks
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
; Pascal source: src/checks.pas

; _p24p_bounds_check ( index low high -- )
; Array bounds violation handler. Called by compiler-generated
; array access code. If index < low or index > high, prints
; "BOUNDS" to UART and halts.
.proc _p24p_bounds_check 0
    loada 2              ; load index (first pushed)
    loada 1              ; load low (second pushed)
    lt                   ; index < low?
    jnz bc_fail
    loada 2              ; load index
    loada 0              ; load high (last pushed)
    gt                   ; index > high?
    jnz bc_fail
    ret 3                ; in range, return normally
bc_fail:
    ; print "BOUNDS\n"
    push 66              ; 'B'
    sys 1
    push 79              ; 'O'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 68              ; 'D'
    sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
.end

; _p24p_nil_check ( ptr -- )
; Nil pointer dereference handler. Called before pointer
; dereference. If ptr = 0, prints "NIL" and halts.
; Otherwise returns with ptr still on stack for use.
.proc _p24p_nil_check 0
    loada 0              ; load ptr
    jnz nc_ok            ; non-nil, safe
    ; print "NIL\n"
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 73              ; 'I'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
nc_ok:
    loada 0              ; return ptr for caller to use
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Read support
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Pascal source: src/read.pas

; _p24p_read_char ( -- c )
; Read single character from UART via sys 2 (GETC).
.proc _p24p_read_char 0
    sys 2                ; GETC -> push char
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_read_int ( -- n )
; Read signed integer from UART. Skips leading whitespace,
; handles optional +/- sign, accumulates decimal digits.
; local0 = n (accumulator), local1 = neg flag, local2 = current char
.proc _p24p_read_int 3
    push 0
    storel 0             ; n = 0
    push 0
    storel 1             ; neg = 0
    ; read first char, skip whitespace
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
ri_ws:
    loadl 2
    push 32              ; space
    eq
    jnz ri_skip
    loadl 2
    push 9               ; tab
    eq
    jnz ri_skip
    jmp ri_sign
ri_skip:
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_ws
ri_sign:
    ; check for minus
    loadl 2
    push 45              ; '-'
    ne
    jnz ri_plus
    push 1
    storel 1             ; neg = 1
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_digits
ri_plus:
    ; check for plus
    loadl 2
    push 43              ; '+'
    ne
    jnz ri_digits
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
ri_digits:
    ; check if c is a digit (48..57)
    loadl 2
    push 48
    lt
    jnz ri_negate        ; c < '0', stop
    loadl 2
    push 57
    gt
    jnz ri_negate        ; c > '9', stop
    ; n = n * 10 + (c - 48)
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mul
    loadl 2
    push 48
    sub
    add
    storel 0             ; n = n * 10 + digit
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_digits
ri_negate:
    loadl 1
    jz ri_done
    loadl 0
    neg
    storel 0             ; n = -n
ri_done:
    loadl 0              ; push result
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_read_ln ( -- )
; Consume characters from UART until LF (10) is read.
.proc _p24p_read_ln 1
    sys 2
    storel 0             ; c = getc
rl_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 10              ; LF
    eq
    jnz rl_done
    sys 2
    storel 0             ; c = getc
    jmp rl_loop
rl_done:
    ret 0
.end

; Phase 2: Heap management
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports pointer/record compilation.
; Pascal source: src/heap.pas

; Globals for heap tracking
; _h_ac = allocation count, _h_fc = free count
; _h_pt = pointer tracking table (16 slots)
.global _h_ac 1
.global _h_fc 1
.global _h_pt 16

; _p24p_heap_init ( -- )
; Initialize heap tracking. Zero counters and pointer table.
.proc _p24p_heap_init 1
    push 0
    storeg _h_ac            ; alloc_count = 0
    push 0
    storeg _h_fc            ; free_count = 0
    ; zero tracking table
    push 0
    storel 0                ; i = 0
hi_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 16
    ge
    jnz hi_done             ; if i >= 16, done
    ; compute address of _h_pt[i] and store 0
    push 0
    addrg _h_pt             ; base address of table
    loadl 0                 ; i
    push 3
    mul                     ; i * 3 (byte offset)
    add                     ; base + i*3
    store                   ; mem[base + i*3] = 0
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; i++
    jmp hi_loop
hi_done:
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_new ( size -- addr )
; Allocate size words via sys 4 (ALLOC). Track pointer.
.proc _p24p_new 2
    loada 0                 ; load size
    sys 4                   ; ALLOC -> addr on stack
    storel 0                ; local0 = addr
    ; alloc_count++
    loadg _h_ac
    push 1
    add
    storeg _h_ac
    ; find empty slot in tracking table
    push 0
    storel 1                ; i = 0
nw_loop:
    loadl 1
    push 16
    ge
    jnz nw_done             ; table full, skip tracking
    ; load _h_pt[i]
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 1
    push 3
    mul
    add
    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
    jnz nw_next             ; slot occupied, try next
    ; store addr in empty slot
    loadl 0                 ; addr
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 1
    push 3
    mul
    add
    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = addr
    jmp nw_done
nw_next:
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; i++
    jmp nw_loop
nw_done:
    loadl 0                 ; push addr as return value
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_dispose ( ptr -- )
; Free heap memory via sys 5 (FREE). Remove from tracking.
.proc _p24p_dispose 1
    loada 0                 ; load ptr
    sys 5                   ; FREE
    ; free_count++
    loadg _h_fc
    push 1
    add
    storeg _h_fc
    ; find and remove ptr from tracking table
    push 0
    storel 0                ; i = 0
dp_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 16
    ge
    jnz dp_done             ; not found, done
    ; load _h_pt[i]
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 0
    push 3
    mul
    add
    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
    loada 0                 ; ptr
    ne
    jnz dp_next             ; not this one
    ; clear the slot
    push 0
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 0
    push 3
    mul
    add
    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = 0
    jmp dp_done
dp_next:
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; i++
    jmp dp_loop
dp_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_leak_report ( -- )
; Report unfreed allocations. Prints "LEAK:N" or "OK:0".
.proc _p24p_leak_report 1
    loadg _h_ac
    loadg _h_fc
    sub
    storel 0                ; leaks = alloc_count - free_count
    loadl 0
    push 0
    gt
    jz lr_ok
    ; print "LEAK:"
    push 76                 ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 69                 ; 'E'
    sys 1
    push 65                 ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 75                 ; 'K'
    sys 1
    push 58                 ; ':'
    sys 1
    loadl 0                 ; push leak count
    jmp lr_print
lr_ok:
    ; print "OK:"
    push 79                 ; 'O'
    sys 1
    push 75                 ; 'K'
    sys 1
    push 58                 ; ':'
    sys 1
    push 0                  ; push 0
lr_print:
    ; print the number using write_int logic inline
    ; (can't call _p24p_write_int from here since it uses ret 1)
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz lr_pos
    push 45
    sys 1
    neg
lr_pos:
    storel 0
    push 0                  ; sentinel
lr_ext:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add                     ; digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz lr_ext
lr_prt:
    dup
    jz lr_end
    sys 1
    jmp lr_prt
lr_end:
    drop                    ; discard sentinel
    push 10                 ; LF
    sys 1
    ret 0
.end

.endmodule

; Phase 2: Write formatting
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
; Pascal source: src/write_fmt.pas

; _p24p_write_char ( c -- )
; Write single character to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_char 0
    loada 0              ; load c
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_int_w ( n width -- )
; Write integer n right-justified in field of given width.
; Counts digits+sign, pads with spaces, then delegates to write_int logic.
.proc _p24p_write_int_w 3
    ; local0 = tmp, local1 = digits, local2 = neg
    push 0
    storel 2                ; neg = 0
    loada 0                 ; load n
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz iw_pos
    push 1
    storel 2                ; neg = 1
    neg                     ; tmp = -n
    jmp iw_count
iw_pos:
    ; tmp = n (already on stack)
iw_count:
    storel 0                ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0
    storel 1                ; digits = 0
iw_dloop:
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; digits++
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0                ; tmp = tmp / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz iw_dloop            ; while tmp != 0
    ; digits += neg
    loadl 1
    loadl 2
    add
    storel 1                ; digits = digits + neg
    ; pad with spaces: while digits < width
iw_pad:
    loadl 1
    loada 1                 ; width
    ge
    jnz iw_num              ; if digits >= width, done padding
    push 32                 ; space
    sys 1
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; digits++ (count padding)
    jmp iw_pad
iw_num:
    ; now print the number using write_int logic inline
    loada 0                 ; load n
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz iw_npos
    push 45                 ; '-'
    sys 1
    neg
iw_npos:
    storel 0                ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0                  ; sentinel
iw_ext:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add                     ; digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz iw_ext
iw_prt:
    dup
    jz iw_end
    sys 1
    jmp iw_prt
iw_end:
    drop                    ; discard sentinel
    ret 2
.end

; _p24p_write_char_w ( c width -- )
; Write character c right-justified in field of given width.
.proc _p24p_write_char_w 1
    loada 1                 ; load width
    storel 0                ; local0 = remaining
cw_pad:
    loadl 0
    push 1
    le
    jnz cw_emit             ; if remaining <= 1, done padding
    push 32                 ; space
    sys 1
    loadl 0
    push 1
    sub
    storel 0                ; remaining--
    jmp cw_pad
cw_emit:
    loada 0                 ; load c
    sys 1                   ; PUTC
    ret 2
.end

; _p24p_write_bool_w ( b width -- )
; Write boolean right-justified in field of given width.
; TRUE=4 chars, FALSE=5 chars.
.proc _p24p_write_bool_w 2
    ; local0 = len, local1 = remaining width
    loada 0                 ; load b
    jz bw_false_len
    push 4
    jmp bw_have_len
bw_false_len:
    push 5
bw_have_len:
    storel 0                ; len
    loada 1                 ; width
    storel 1
bw_pad:
    loadl 0
    loadl 1
    ge
    jnz bw_emit             ; if len >= width, done
    push 32
    sys 1
    loadl 1
    push 1
    sub
    storel 1                ; width--
    jmp bw_pad
bw_emit:
    loada 0                 ; load b
    call _p24p_write_bool
    ret 2
.end

; _p24p_write_str_w ( addr width -- )
; Write string right-justified in field of given width.
; First counts string length, then pads and prints.
.proc _p24p_write_str_w 2
    ; local0 = len, local1 = ptr
    push 0
    storel 0                ; len = 0
    loada 0                 ; load addr
    storel 1                ; ptr = addr
sw_count:
    loadl 1
    loadb                   ; byte at ptr
    jz sw_pad_start         ; null terminator -> done counting
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; len++
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; ptr++
    jmp sw_count
sw_pad_start:
    loadl 0
    loada 1                 ; width
    ge
    jnz sw_emit             ; if len >= width, no padding
    push 32
    sys 1
    loada 1
    push 1
    sub
    storea 1                ; width-- (modify arg for loop)
    jmp sw_pad_start
sw_emit:
    loada 0                 ; addr
    call _p24p_write_str
    ret 2
.end

; Phase 2: I/O state functions
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Pascal source: src/io_state.pas

; Globals for I/O lookahead buffer
; _io_la = lookahead char (-1 = empty)
; _io_ef = eof flag (0 = not eof, 1 = eof)
.global _io_la 1
.global _io_ef 1

; _p24p_eof ( -- flag )
; Returns 1 if at end of input (EOT byte 0x04), 0 otherwise.
; Uses a one-character lookahead buffer.
.proc _p24p_eof 0
    loadg _io_la
    push 0
    lt
    jz ef_have              ; if lookahead >= 0, we have a buffered char
    ; buffer empty, read a char
    sys 2                   ; GETC
    storeg _io_la           ; store in lookahead
ef_have:
    loadg _io_la
    push 4                  ; EOT
    eq                      ; lookahead == EOT?
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_eoln ( -- flag )
; Returns 1 if at end of line (LF byte 10) or at EOF.
; Uses same lookahead buffer as eof.
.proc _p24p_eoln 0
    loadg _io_la
    push 0
    lt
    jz el_have
    sys 2                   ; GETC
    storeg _io_la
el_have:
    loadg _io_la
    push 10                 ; LF
    eq
    jnz el_true
    loadg _io_la
    push 4                  ; EOT
    eq
    jnz el_true
    push 0                  ; not at eoln
    ret 0
el_true:
    push 1
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_subrange_check ( value low high -- )
; Subrange violation handler. Like bounds_check but prints "RANGE".
.proc _p24p_subrange_check 0
    loada 2              ; load value (first pushed)
    loada 1              ; load low
    lt                   ; value < low?
    jnz sr_fail
    loada 2              ; load value
    loada 0              ; load high
    gt                   ; value > high?
    jnz sr_fail
    ret 3                ; in range, return normally
sr_fail:
    push 82              ; 'R'
    sys 1
    push 65              ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 71              ; 'G'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
.end

.endmodule

.module runtime
.export _p24p_write_int
.export _p24p_write_bool
.export _p24p_write_ln
.export _p24p_write_str
.export _p24p_abs
.export _p24p_odd
.export _p24p_ord
.export _p24p_chr
.export _p24p_succ
.export _p24p_pred
.export _p24p_sqr
.export _p24p_bounds_check
.export _p24p_nil_check
.export _p24p_read_int
.export _p24p_read_char
.export _p24p_read_ln
.export _p24p_heap_init
.export _p24p_new
.export _p24p_dispose
.export _p24p_leak_report
.export _p24p_write_char
.export _p24p_write_int_w
.export _p24p_write_char_w
.export _p24p_write_bool_w
.export _p24p_write_str_w
.export _p24p_eof
.export _p24p_eoln
.export _p24p_subrange_check

; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; n '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
wi_print:
    dup
    jz wi_done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp wi_print
wi_done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_bool ( b -- )
; Print TRUE or FALSE to UART.
; Compares argument to 0: if zero prints FALSE, otherwise prints TRUE.
; Each character is pushed and output via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_bool 0
    loada 0              ; load argument b
    jz wb_false
    ; print "TRUE"
    push 84              ; 'T'
    sys 1
    push 82              ; 'R'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
    jmp wb_done
wb_false:
    ; print "FALSE"
    push 70              ; 'F'
    sys 1
    push 65              ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
wb_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_ln ( -- )
; Print newline (LF) to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_ln 0
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_write_str ( addr -- )
; Print null-terminated string from data segment to UART.
; Walks bytes via loadb, calls sys 1 (PUTC) for each until null.
.proc _p24p_write_str 1
    loada 0              ; load string address
    storel 0             ; local0 = pointer
ws_loop:
    loadl 0              ; load pointer
    loadb                ; load byte at pointer
    dup
    jz ws_done           ; null terminator -> done
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0             ; pointer++
    jmp ws_loop
ws_done:
    drop                 ; discard null byte
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Standard functions
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Will be replaced with Pascal-compiled versions when p24c Phase 1 lands.

; _p24p_abs ( x -- |x| )
; Absolute value. Returns x if x >= 0, else -x.
.proc _p24p_abs 0
    loada 0              ; load x
    dup
    push 0
    lt                   ; x < 0?
    jz abs_done
    neg                  ; negate if negative
abs_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_odd ( x -- x mod 2 <> 0 )
; Returns true (1) if x is odd, false (0) if even.
.proc _p24p_odd 0
    loada 0              ; load x
    push 2
    mod                  ; x mod 2
    push 0
    ne                   ; result != 0 -> true
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_ord ( c -- c )
; Character to integer. Identity on this VM (chars are integers).
.proc _p24p_ord 0
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_chr ( n -- n )
; Integer to character. Identity on this VM (chars are integers).
.proc _p24p_chr 0
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_succ ( x -- x+1 )
; Next ordinal value.
.proc _p24p_succ 0
    loada 0
    push 1
    add
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_pred ( x -- x-1 )
; Previous ordinal value.
.proc _p24p_pred 0
    loada 0
    push 1
    sub
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_sqr ( x -- x*x )
; Square of integer.
.proc _p24p_sqr 0
    loada 0
    dup
    mul
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Runtime checks
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
; Pascal source: src/checks.pas

; _p24p_bounds_check ( index low high -- )
; Array bounds violation handler. Called by compiler-generated
; array access code. If index < low or index > high, prints
; "BOUNDS" to UART and halts.
.proc _p24p_bounds_check 0
    loada 2              ; load index (first pushed)
    loada 1              ; load low (second pushed)
    lt                   ; index < low?
    jnz bc_fail
    loada 2              ; load index
    loada 0              ; load high (last pushed)
    gt                   ; index > high?
    jnz bc_fail
    ret 3                ; in range, return normally
bc_fail:
    ; print "BOUNDS\n"
    push 66              ; 'B'
    sys 1
    push 79              ; 'O'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 68              ; 'D'
    sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
.end

; _p24p_nil_check ( ptr -- )
; Nil pointer dereference handler. Called before pointer
; dereference. If ptr = 0, prints "NIL" and halts.
; Otherwise returns with ptr still on stack for use.
.proc _p24p_nil_check 0
    loada 0              ; load ptr
    jnz nc_ok            ; non-nil, safe
    ; print "NIL\n"
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 73              ; 'I'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
nc_ok:
    loada 0              ; return ptr for caller to use
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Read support
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Pascal source: src/read.pas

; _p24p_read_char ( -- c )
; Read single character from UART via sys 2 (GETC).
.proc _p24p_read_char 0
    sys 2                ; GETC -> push char
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_read_int ( -- n )
; Read signed integer from UART. Skips leading whitespace,
; handles optional +/- sign, accumulates decimal digits.
; local0 = n (accumulator), local1 = neg flag, local2 = current char
.proc _p24p_read_int 3
    push 0
    storel 0             ; n = 0
    push 0
    storel 1             ; neg = 0
    ; read first char, skip whitespace
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
ri_ws:
    loadl 2
    push 32              ; space
    eq
    jnz ri_skip
    loadl 2
    push 9               ; tab
    eq
    jnz ri_skip
    jmp ri_sign
ri_skip:
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_ws
ri_sign:
    ; check for minus
    loadl 2
    push 45              ; '-'
    ne
    jnz ri_plus
    push 1
    storel 1             ; neg = 1
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_digits
ri_plus:
    ; check for plus
    loadl 2
    push 43              ; '+'
    ne
    jnz ri_digits
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
ri_digits:
    ; check if c is a digit (48..57)
    loadl 2
    push 48
    lt
    jnz ri_negate        ; c < '0', stop
    loadl 2
    push 57
    gt
    jnz ri_negate        ; c > '9', stop
    ; n = n * 10 + (c - 48)
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mul
    loadl 2
    push 48
    sub
    add
    storel 0             ; n = n * 10 + digit
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_digits
ri_negate:
    loadl 1
    jz ri_done
    loadl 0
    neg
    storel 0             ; n = -n
ri_done:
    loadl 0              ; push result
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_read_ln ( -- )
; Consume characters from UART until LF (10) is read.
.proc _p24p_read_ln 1
    sys 2
    storel 0             ; c = getc
rl_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 10              ; LF
    eq
    jnz rl_done
    sys 2
    storel 0             ; c = getc
    jmp rl_loop
rl_done:
    ret 0
.end

; Phase 2: Heap management
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports pointer/record compilation.
; Pascal source: src/heap.pas

; Globals for heap tracking
; _h_ac = allocation count, _h_fc = free count
; _h_pt = pointer tracking table (16 slots)
.global _h_ac 1
.global _h_fc 1
.global _h_pt 16

; _p24p_heap_init ( -- )
; Initialize heap tracking. Zero counters and pointer table.
.proc _p24p_heap_init 1
    push 0
    storeg _h_ac            ; alloc_count = 0
    push 0
    storeg _h_fc            ; free_count = 0
    ; zero tracking table
    push 0
    storel 0                ; i = 0
hi_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 16
    ge
    jnz hi_done             ; if i >= 16, done
    ; compute address of _h_pt[i] and store 0
    push 0
    addrg _h_pt             ; base address of table
    loadl 0                 ; i
    push 3
    mul                     ; i * 3 (byte offset)
    add                     ; base + i*3
    store                   ; mem[base + i*3] = 0
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; i++
    jmp hi_loop
hi_done:
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_new ( size -- addr )
; Allocate size words via sys 4 (ALLOC). Track pointer.
.proc _p24p_new 2
    loada 0                 ; load size
    sys 4                   ; ALLOC -> addr on stack
    storel 0                ; local0 = addr
    ; alloc_count++
    loadg _h_ac
    push 1
    add
    storeg _h_ac
    ; find empty slot in tracking table
    push 0
    storel 1                ; i = 0
nw_loop:
    loadl 1
    push 16
    ge
    jnz nw_done             ; table full, skip tracking
    ; load _h_pt[i]
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 1
    push 3
    mul
    add
    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
    jnz nw_next             ; slot occupied, try next
    ; store addr in empty slot
    loadl 0                 ; addr
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 1
    push 3
    mul
    add
    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = addr
    jmp nw_done
nw_next:
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; i++
    jmp nw_loop
nw_done:
    loadl 0                 ; push addr as return value
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_dispose ( ptr -- )
; Free heap memory via sys 5 (FREE). Remove from tracking.
.proc _p24p_dispose 1
    loada 0                 ; load ptr
    sys 5                   ; FREE
    ; free_count++
    loadg _h_fc
    push 1
    add
    storeg _h_fc
    ; find and remove ptr from tracking table
    push 0
    storel 0                ; i = 0
dp_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 16
    ge
    jnz dp_done             ; not found, done
    ; load _h_pt[i]
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 0
    push 3
    mul
    add
    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
    loada 0                 ; ptr
    ne
    jnz dp_next             ; not this one
    ; clear the slot
    push 0
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 0
    push 3
    mul
    add
    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = 0
    jmp dp_done
dp_next:
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; i++
    jmp dp_loop
dp_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_leak_report ( -- )
; Report unfreed allocations. Prints "LEAK:N" or "OK:0".
.proc _p24p_leak_report 1
    loadg _h_ac
    loadg _h_fc
    sub
    storel 0                ; leaks = alloc_count - free_count
    loadl 0
    push 0
    gt
    jz lr_ok
    ; print "LEAK:"
    push 76                 ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 69                 ; 'E'
    sys 1
    push 65                 ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 75                 ; 'K'
    sys 1
    push 58                 ; ':'
    sys 1
    loadl 0                 ; push leak count
    jmp lr_print
lr_ok:
    ; print "OK:"
    push 79                 ; 'O'
    sys 1
    push 75                 ; 'K'
    sys 1
    push 58                 ; ':'
    sys 1
    push 0                  ; push 0
lr_print:
    ; print the number using write_int logic inline
    ; (can't call _p24p_write_int from here since it uses ret 1)
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz lr_pos
    push 45
    sys 1
    neg
lr_pos:
    storel 0
    push 0                  ; sentinel
lr_ext:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add                     ; digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz lr_ext
lr_prt:
    dup
    jz lr_end
    sys 1
    jmp lr_prt
lr_end:
    drop                    ; discard sentinel
    push 10                 ; LF
    sys 1
    ret 0
.end

.endmodule

lines: +; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
+; Write single character to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
+; Write integer n right-justified in field of given width.
+; Counts digits+sign, pads with spaces, then delegates to write_int logic.
+    ; local0 = tmp, local1 = digits, local2 = neg
+    ; pad with spaces: while digits < width
+    jnz iw_num              ; if digits >= width, done padding
+    ; now print the number using write_int logic inline
+; Write character c right-justified in field of given width.
+    jnz cw_emit             ; if remaining <= 1, done padding
+; Write boolean right-justified in field of given width.
+    jnz bw_emit             ; if len >= width, done
+; Write string right-justified in field of given width.
+; First counts string length, then pads and prints.
+    jz sw_pad_start         ; null terminator -> done counting
+    jnz sw_emit             ; if len >= width, no padding
+; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
+; Returns 1 if at end of input (EOT byte 0x04), 0 otherwise.
+    jz ef_have              ; if lookahead >= 0, we have a buffered char
+; Returns 1 if at end of line (LF byte 10) or at EOF.
+; Uses same lookahead buffer as eof.
+; Subrange violation handler. Like bounds_check but prints "RANGE".

content.text:
I see the bug. In the digit-counting loop, `storel 0` is used for `tmp = tmp / 10`, which destroys local0. Then after counting, `loada 0` loads the *original argument* n (42), but the number printing loop reuses `storel 0` again. The problem is that the digit counting modifies local0, then the padding loop increments local1 (digits) to match width, so when we print, local1 may be wrong for calculating the actual number of digits.

Wait, actually the issue is simpler. Look at the padding loop — it increments `local1` (digits) until `digits >= width`. For 42 (2 digits) with width 8, it pads 6 spaces but also increments digits to 8. Then the number printing stores to local0 — that's fine since we reload from `loada 0` (the argument). Let me trace through more carefully...

Actually, the real issue is: after the digit-counting loop, `local0 = 0` (tmp was divided down to 0). But the number-printing section reuses local0 for the same purpose (it stores `abs(n)` there). Let me trace:

1. Count digits: local0 starts as abs(42)=42, divided to 0. digits=2.
2. Pad: 6 spaces printed, digits incremented to 8.
3. Print: `loada 0` = 42. Check sign, store to local0. Extract digits: 42%10=2+48='2', 42/10=4, store local0=4. 4%10=4+48='4', 4/10=0, store local0=0. Print: '4' then '2'. Output should be "42".

Hmm that should work. But the output shows just "8"... Let me look more carefully at the actual output. The line says `                                         8` which is a lot of spaces then `8`. Width 8 should give 6 spaces then `42`.

Wait — is the problem that `loada 1` for the width argument is wrong? In the .proc declaration `iw 3`, it has 3 locals. Arguments are pushed before the call. With `push 42; push 8; call iw`, on the stack: ..., 42, 8, return_addr, saved_fp. So `loada 0` = 8 (last pushed arg, width!) and `loada 1` = 42 (n). The argument order is reversed from what I wrote!

The p-code VM pushes arguments left-to-right but `loada 0` accesses the *last* pushed argument. So the call `push 42; push 8; call iw` means `loada 0` = 8, `loada 1` = 42.

Let me fix the test to swap argument order, OR fix the procedure to match the calling convention.

Looking at `_p24p_write_int` in runtime.spc — it takes 1 arg and uses `loada 0` for n. For 2-arg procedures like bounds_check, `loada 2` is the first pushed (index), `loada 0` is the last pushed (high). So the convention is: **loada 0 = last arg pushed, loada N = first arg pushed**.

For `write_int_w(n, width)`, the compiler will push n first, then width. So `loada 0` = width, `loada 1` = n. Let me fix the .spc:

   588→    sys 1                ; PUTC

   594→; Counts digits+sign, pads with spaces, then delegates to write_int logic.
   595→.proc _p24p_write_int_w 3
   596→    ; local0 = tmp, local1 = digits, local2 = neg
   597→    push 0
   598→    storel 2                ; neg = 0
   599→    loada 0                 ; load n
   600→    dup
   601→    push 0

   606→    neg                     ; tmp = -n

   612→    push 0

   614→iw_dloop:

   616→    push 1

file.content:
; _p24p_write_char ( c -- )
; Write single character to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_char 0
    loada 0              ; load c
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_int_w ( n width -- )
; Write integer n right-justified in field of given width.
; Counts digits+sign, pads with spaces, then delegates to write_int logic.
.proc _p24p_write_int_w 3
    ; local0 = tmp, local1 = digits, local2 = neg
    push 0
    storel 2                ; neg = 0
    loada 0                 ; load n
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz iw_pos
    push 1
    storel 2                ; neg = 1
    neg                     ; tmp = -n
    jmp iw_count
iw_pos:
    ; tmp = n (already on stack)
iw_count:
    storel 0                ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0
    storel 1                ; digits = 0
iw_dloop:
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; digits++
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0                ; tmp = tmp / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz iw_dloop            ; while tmp != 0
    ; digits += neg
    loadl 1
    loadl 2
    add
    storel 1                ; digits = digits + neg
    ; pad with spaces: while digits < width
iw_pad:
    loadl 1
    loada 1                 ; width
    ge
    jnz iw_num              ; if digits >= width, done padding
    push 32                 ; space
    sys 1
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; digits++ (count padding)
    jmp iw_pad
iw_num:
    ; now print the number using write_int logic inline
    loada 0                 ; load n
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz iw_npos
    push 45                 ; '-'
    sys 1
    neg
iw_npos:
    storel 0                ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0                  ; sentinel
iw_ext:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add                     ; digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz iw_ext
iw_prt:
    dup
    jz iw_end
    sys 1
    jmp iw_prt

content.text: I need to swap `loada 0` and `loada 1` throughout write_int_w, write_char_w, write_bool_w, and write_str_w. The convention is `loada 0` = last pushed = width, `loada 1` = first pushed = value.
